data.report_title: Supply Chain Decision-Making Topology: Diagnostic Brief — US Automotive OEM (Anonymized)
data.executive_summary: This diagnostic brief synthesizes five interviews spanning VP, Director, Senior Manager, and Manager seniority levels across Overall Supply Chain Leadership, Direct Materials Procurement, Production Planning, Metals Commodity Purchasing, and Plant-Level Supply Chain execution. The interviews reveal a function with coherent formal governance architecture — tiered meetings, defined KPIs, and broadly understood escalation thresholds — but with material gaps in cross-functional decision authority, inconsistent follow-through on escalated decisions, and a monthly S&OP working session that structurally underperforms its intended role. The top priority for further investigation is the ambiguous ownership and chronic underperformance of the mid-level cross-functional S&OP forum, which is the intended load-bearing mechanism for demand-supply alignment but is currently functioning as an ad hoc bilateral workaround network.

table.name: Patricia Chen
table.direct_reports: 6 Directors (Procurement, Supplier Quality, Logistics, Production Planning, Inventory Management, Strategy & Analytics)
table.scope_summary: End-to-end supply chain accountability from upstream sourcing through plant-floor delivery; owns top-level KPIs and chairs monthly Supply Chain Leadership Meeting and quarterly Executive S&OP.

table.name: David Okonkwo
table.scope_summary: Owns ~$2B annual production materials spend across ~200 suppliers; accountable for landed cost, supplier OTD, and first-pass quality rate.

table.name: Sarah Martinez
table.direct_reports: 6 planners (master scheduling, materials planning, demand planning)
table.scope_summary: Owns master production schedule, MRP process, and S&OP facilitation; accountable for schedule attainment, shortage incidents, and inventory days on hand.

table.name: Marcus Thompson
table.scope_summary: Manages ~$600M annual metals spend (steel, aluminum) across 35 suppliers; accountable for unit cost, supplier on-time delivery, and scrap/waste minimization.

table.name: James Patel
table.role: Plant Supply Chain Manager (Lead Assembly Plant)
table.reports_to: Director of Operations (plant-side); functional dotted line to central Supply Chain implied
table.direct_reports: Team of 8 (inbound receipt, inventory control, local supplier coordination, shortage escalation)
table.scope_summary: Owns plant-floor materials execution including on-time staging, inventory accuracy, and shortage escalation; operates at the interface between central supply chain decisions and local manufacturing reality.

section_2_kpi_ownership_matrix.title: Section 2: KPI Ownership Matrix

kpis.sole_or_shared: ⚠️ Shared — Patricia owns the levers; Finance owns measurement. Allocation methodology creates recurring tension.
kpis.meeting_where_reviewed: Supply Chain Leadership Meeting; Executive S&OP (quarterly)
kpis.decision_it_drives: Sourcing strategy adjustments, contract renegotiation triggers, cost-reduction program prioritization

kpis.owners: Patricia Chen (accountable); Director, Supplier Quality (operational owner — not interviewed)
kpis.sole_or_shared: Sole (supply chain side); clearest KPI Patricia named as cleanly owned
kpis.decision_it_drives: Supplier corrective action, controlled shipping authorization, disqualification escalation

kpis.owners: Patricia Chen; Manufacturing VP (plant schedule side)
kpis.sole_or_shared: ⚠️ Shared — Patricia owns inbound logistics and planning side; Manufacturing owns plant schedule changes that can cause misses
kpis.meeting_where_reviewed: Supply Chain Leadership Meeting; Executive S&OP
kpis.decision_it_drives: Logistics network adjustments, carrier escalation, schedule recovery actions

kpis.owners: Patricia Chen; Sales (demand signal); Manufacturing (schedule stability)
kpis.sole_or_shared: ⚠️ Shared — Patricia owns supply-side levers only; corrective action requires cross-functional alignment with Sales and Manufacturing
kpis.meeting_where_reviewed: Supply Chain Leadership Meeting; Executive S&OP
kpis.decision_it_drives: Safety stock policy changes, demand plan adjustments, supply-side buffer reductions

kpis.meeting_where_reviewed: Supply Chain Leadership Meeting; Monthly Supplier Performance Review
kpis.decision_it_drives: Contract negotiation, sourcing award decisions, cost-reduction program initiation

kpis.owners: David Okonkwo (supplier side); Sarah Martinez (planning/forecast side)
kpis.sole_or_shared: ⚠️ Shared — root cause attribution between supplier execution and forecast quality is a recurring contested point
kpis.review_frequency: Bi-weekly (Materials Planning Sync); Monthly (Supplier Performance Review)
kpis.meeting_where_reviewed: Bi-weekly Materials Planning Sync; Monthly Supplier Performance Review
kpis.decision_it_drives: Corrective action plans, incoming inspection level changes, dual-sourcing authorization

kpis.owners: David Okonkwo (supplier side); Supplier Quality Director (standards & audits)
kpis.sole_or_shared: ⚠️ Shared — Procurement owns supplier selection and contract terms; Supplier Quality owns measurement and disposition
kpis.decision_it_drives: Supplier corrective action, PPAP holds, incoming inspection adjustments

kpis.meeting_where_reviewed: Bi-weekly Supplier Performance Review; Weekly Procurement Team Meeting (escalated items)
kpis.decision_it_drives: Spot buy decisions, quarterly price adjustments, contract renewal positioning

kpis.owners: Marcus Thompson (material spec compliance); Quality Engineer (measurement & disposition)
kpis.sole_or_shared: ⚠️ Shared — Marcus influences through supplier selection and spec compliance; Quality owns measurement and formal disposition
kpis.decision_it_drives: Incoming inspection adjustments, supplier corrective action, material deviation dispositions

kpis.owners: Sarah Martinez (nominally sole); Manufacturing (equipment/labor side); Procurement (material delivery side)
kpis.sole_or_shared: ⚠️ Shared in practice — Sarah owns the number on paper but levers are distributed across Manufacturing and Procurement
kpis.decision_it_drives: Sequence changes, line balancing, short-term expedite authorizations

kpis.owners: Sarah Martinez (plant-level count); James Patel (plant execution); central Supply Chain (aggregate)
kpis.sole_or_shared: ⚠️ Shared across three levels — plant, planning, and central — with no single named accountable owner for aggregate trend management
kpis.meeting_where_reviewed: Daily Production Stand-Up; Bi-weekly Materials Planning Sync; Monthly S&OP
kpis.decision_it_drives: Expedite authorization, safety stock adjustments, shortage escalation to VP

kpis.owners: Sarah Martinez (primary); Sales (demand signal); Manufacturing (schedule stability)
kpis.sole_or_shared: ⚠️ Shared — Sarah has most genuine control via safety stock and release decisions, but Sales-driven demand swings can override
kpis.meeting_where_reviewed: Monthly S&OP; Supply Chain Leadership Meeting
kpis.decision_it_drives: Safety stock policy, material release cadence, inventory positioning decisions

kpis.owners: James Patel (execution); Sarah Martinez (demand signal/plan changes)
kpis.sole_or_shared: ⚠️ Shared — James owns physical execution; late plan changes from central Planning cause misses that appear on James's scorecard
kpis.meeting_where_reviewed: Daily 7am Production Stand-Up; Bi-weekly Materials Planning Sync
kpis.decision_it_drives: Local expedite requests, staging resequencing, shortage escalation

kpis.sole_or_shared: Sole — measured via cycle counts, locally controlled
kpis.review_frequency: Ongoing cycle counts; reviewed at weekly Plant Operations Meeting
kpis.decision_it_drives: Cycle count frequency adjustments, warehouse process corrections

section_2_kpi_ownership_matrix.kpi_deduplication_notes: OTD appears across three sub-functions under different labels: 'On-Time Delivery to Plants' (Patricia/central), 'Supplier On-Time Delivery' (David/Procurement), and 'On-Time Material Delivery to Production' (James/Plant). These are related but distinct metrics measuring different points in the delivery chain — they have not been consolidated as they represent genuinely different measurement points and owners. 'Shortage Incidents' is tracked at both plant level (James) and aggregate central level (Sarah/Patricia) — flagged as having no single named owner for the aggregate trend.

section_3_meeting_architecture.title: Section 3: Meeting Architecture
section_3_meeting_architecture.note: Meetings are deduplicated and canonically named. The 'Bi-weekly Materials Planning Sync' is confirmed as the same forum referenced by David Okonkwo ('twice-weekly Materials Planning sync'), Sarah Martinez ('bi-weekly Materials Planning sync with central'), James Patel ('bi-weekly Materials Planning sync with central'), and Marcus Thompson ('bi-weekly supplier reviews' — partially overlapping but distinct; see note). The 'Daily Production Stand-Up' is confirmed as the same 7am forum referenced by Sarah Martinez and James Patel.

meetings.confirmed_attendees: Patricia Chen (VP, Supply Chain); COO; CFO; Sales leadership; Manufacturing VP; Sarah Martinez (as SME resource, not facilitator)
meetings.purpose: Review and sanction the strategic planning framework — capacity expansion, major supplier contracts, network changes, capital decisions. Framework-level governance, not operational gap resolution.
meetings.lean_governance_note: Structured executive review consistent with tiered management principles; functions as the apex governance body for horizon-3 supply chain decisions.
meetings.decision_types_made: Capital allocation approvals; major network restructuring decisions; strategic supplier commitments beyond VP authority; planning horizon framework changes
meetings.key_outputs: Sanctioned plans and capital approvals cascaded to monthly S&OP; strategic direction changes
meetings.who_carries_out_actions: Patricia Chen and her Directors; cross-functional VPs within their own functions

meetings.confirmed_attendees: Patricia Chen (VP, Supply Chain); Sales VP; Manufacturing VP; Finance; Director, Supply Chain Strategy & Analytics (represents SC function per Patricia); Sarah Martinez (facilitator/process owner)
meetings.purpose: Finalize demand plan, resolve demand-supply gaps within established planning framework, authorize tactical inventory and capacity decisions. Intended as mid-level cross-functional decision forum.
meetings.forum_type: ⚠️ Intended as true decision forum; functioning inconsistently — Patricia describes it as underperforming when under pressure, with alignment work migrating to bilateral calls
meetings.lean_governance_note: Structurally resembles an Obeya-style integrated planning review but lacks the stable attendance and clear chair accountability required for lean governance effectiveness.
meetings.decision_types_made: Demand plan finalization; supply-demand gap resolution; tactical inventory positioning; product mix adjustments within sanctioned plan. Patricia closes contested decisions.
meetings.key_outputs: Finalized monthly demand plan; inventory positioning decisions; escalation items for Executive S&OP
meetings.who_carries_out_actions: Sarah Martinez (schedule and MRP execution); Procurement Directors (supply-side adjustments); Sales (commercial commitments)
meetings.governance_gap: ⚠️ Chair accountability is ambiguous — Director of Strategy & Analytics sets agenda by default but no explicit chair with accountability for forum effectiveness was ever designated. When under operational pressure, attendance degrades and decision-authority substitutes attend.

meetings.confirmed_attendees: Patricia Chen (chair); all 6 Directors including David Okonkwo and Sarah Martinez; Finance and Operations representation (stated by Marcus Thompson, unconfirmed by directors)
meetings.purpose: Review sub-function KPI performance, close structural supply chain decisions above Director authority, surface and resolve cross-sub-function issues.
meetings.lean_governance_note: Consistent with a lean tier-2/tier-3 management review; Patricia chairs, Directors are accountable nodes.
meetings.decision_types_made: New supplier approvals; contract awards above $50M; significant volume reallocation; supplier disqualification; cross-sub-function issue resolution
meetings.key_outputs: Decisions on structural supplier and sourcing issues; KPI accountability assignments; escalation items for Executive S&OP
meetings.who_carries_out_actions: Relevant Directors own execution within their sub-functions; Patricia owns items requiring cross-functional coordination
meetings.governance_gap: ⚠️ Decision documentation and closure loop noted as inconsistent by Patricia — verbal alignment in the room does not consistently translate into written direction that cascades to execution teams.

meetings.confirmed_attendees: Patricia Chen; Manufacturing VP; Finance; Engineering; COO (Marcus Webb) chairs
meetings.purpose: Near-term operational issue resolution across Supply Chain, Manufacturing, Finance, and Engineering. Patricia describes this as the most action-oriented recurring meeting she attends.
meetings.lean_governance_note: Closest analog to a lean daily/weekly management tiered review at the executive level; cross-functional, chaired by COO, action-oriented.
meetings.decision_types_made: Near-term operational escalations requiring cross-functional authorization; production risk responses; resource prioritization
meetings.key_outputs: Cross-functional action items with owners; operational recovery decisions
meetings.who_carries_out_actions: Respective function VPs and their teams

meetings.confirmed_attendees: David Okonkwo (chair); Supplier Quality team; Production Planning team; Procurement buyers/analysts. Engineering does NOT regularly attend.
meetings.purpose: Review supplier scorecards, resolve root cause attribution disputes, authorize corrective action plans and inspection level changes.
meetings.forum_type: True decision forum for supplier-execution issues; informational/escalation-staging only for items requiring Engineering or contract-level authority
meetings.lean_governance_note: Structured supplier review consistent with lean supplier management cadences; gap is Engineering's absence creates a recurring bottleneck for disposition decisions.
meetings.decision_types_made: Corrective action plan authorization; incoming inspection level changes; root cause attribution; temporary volume shifts within existing contracts
meetings.key_outputs: Corrective action plans assigned to suppliers; escalation items to Supply Chain Leadership Meeting for contract-level decisions
meetings.who_carries_out_actions: David Okonkwo and relevant buyers; Supplier Quality Engineers
meetings.governance_gap: ⚠️ Engineering does not regularly attend. Disposition decisions requiring engineering sign-off (e.g., tolerance relaxations) cannot close in this forum — they are logged and referred to Engineering informally with no SLA or escalation mechanism.

meetings.cadence: Bi-weekly (Monday and Thursday per Sarah Martinez)
meetings.confirmed_attendees: David Okonkwo or senior buyer (Procurement); Sarah Martinez's Planning team; James Patel (plant-side); Marcus Thompson attends a version of this or an overlapping forum for metals-specific issues
meetings.purpose: Near-term supply coverage review, shortage identification, expedite prioritization, and root cause attribution for delivery misses.
meetings.forum_type: True decision forum for tactical supply decisions within existing contracts and authority
meetings.lean_governance_note: High-cadence operational sync consistent with lean tiered daily management at the manager/specialist level; most action-oriented sub-VP forum identified.
meetings.decision_types_made: Expedite authorizations (within authority); allocation priority calls; short-term schedule adjustments; shortage escalation to Director level when required
meetings.key_outputs: Expedite actions; shortage risk flags; escalation items to Directors; premium freight requests (subject to $15K plant-level threshold)
meetings.who_carries_out_actions: Planning Managers, Procurement buyers, James Patel and plant team
meetings.governance_gap: ⚠️ Plant-side premium freight escalations above $15K must route through central Commodity Manager then Procurement Director — approval latency in urgent situations documented as causing near-stoppage events (see James Patel interview).

meetings.confirmed_attendees: Sarah Martinez; Manufacturing supervision; James Patel (plant-side); Manufacturing VP (when significant issues present)
meetings.purpose: Prior-day schedule attainment review, same-day recovery decision-making, shortage and risk flagging for immediate response.
meetings.forum_type: ⚠️ Mixed — immediate sequence/line-balancing decisions are made; broader issues are flagged and routed up. James Patel characterizes it as primarily information-sharing; Sarah Martinez characterizes it as a decision forum for sequencing.
meetings.lean_governance_note: Closest to a lean tier-1 daily management meeting; structured, time-boxed, floor-level. Cadence and format are consistent with TPS-influenced governance.
meetings.decision_types_made: Same-day sequence changes (Sarah's authority, within 2-week window); line balancing between lines; immediate shortage escalation triggers
meetings.key_outputs: Day's production sequence; escalation flags to Materials Planning Sync or directly to VP when line stoppage is imminent
meetings.who_carries_out_actions: Sarah Martinez (schedule execution); Manufacturing supervisors (line execution); James Patel (plant materials staging)

meetings.confirmed_attendees: James Patel; Plant Manager; Director of Operations (plant); Patricia Chen (VP, Supply Chain); Planning Directors or Commodity Managers from central (agenda-dependent); Regional Manufacturing VP (inconsistent)
meetings.purpose: Surface plant-level supply chain performance issues, align on process improvement needs, assign follow-up actions between plant and central.
meetings.forum_type: ⚠️ Primarily informational and alignment-oriented — decisions in principle are made but process change follow-through is chronically incomplete due to absent action ownership
meetings.lean_governance_note: Structurally appropriate as a quarterly plant-central governance bridge, but operates more as a status report forum than a lean accountability review. Lacks the action-closure discipline of effective tiered management.
meetings.decision_types_made: Policy alignment on plant-central interfaces; in-principle authorization of process changes; formal surfacing of structural issues (e.g., $15K premium freight threshold)
meetings.key_outputs: Action items — but with diffuse ownership and no named tracker between quarters
meetings.who_carries_out_actions: ⚠️ Unclear — James Patel tracks plant-side actions; central-side actions have no named owner between quarterly sessions
meetings.governance_gap: ⚠️ No dedicated action-tracking owner between quarters. Items raised (including the $15K premium freight threshold review) have received general agreement but have not been assigned formal owners with dates. This is a structural gap in plant-central governance.

meetings.purpose: Supplier performance updates, in-function decision-making on order placement, escalations, and supplier actions within David's authority.
meetings.lean_governance_note: Standard lean sub-function weekly management review; decisions close in the room for items within Director authority.
meetings.decision_types_made: Order placement and timing; supplier escalation authorizations; corrective action initiation; expedite approvals within David's authority
meetings.key_outputs: Buyer action assignments; escalation items for Supply Chain Leadership Meeting
meetings.who_carries_out_actions: Buyers and analysts per assigned actions

meetings.canonical_name: Weekly 1:1s (Patricia with Direct Reports; Patricia with COO)
meetings.confirmed_attendees: Patricia Chen + each direct report (6 individual sessions); Patricia Chen + COO (Marcus Webb)
meetings.purpose: Pulse checks, proactive loop-ins on issues approaching escalation thresholds, directional alignment before formal decisions.
meetings.forum_type: Informational / soft-escalation forum — not a formal decision venue but functionally substitutes for formal forums when those forums are too slow
meetings.lean_governance_note: Bilateral rather than tiered; Patricia notes variability in structure across her 6 director 1:1s — inconsistency noted as a self-identified development area.
meetings.decision_types_made: No formal decisions close here; directional guidance given that shapes downstream decisions
meetings.key_outputs: VP awareness; informal directional guidance; flags for Weekly Operations Review or Supply Chain Leadership Meeting

section_4_decision_authority_map.title: Section 4: Decision Authority Map

decision_types.decision: Supplier sourcing contracts up to $50M annually
decision_types.forum: Supply Chain Leadership Meeting; bilateral with COO for complex cases
decision_types.clarity: Clear

decision_types.forum: Supply Chain Leadership Meeting; unilateral when speed is required
decision_types.clarity: Clear — Patricia explicitly owns this unilaterally, citing speed imperative

decision_types.forum: Monthly S&OP — Patricia closes contested decisions
decision_types.clarity: Clear within S&OP; ⚠️ ambiguous when S&OP is absent or attendance degrades

decision_types.forum: Bilateral calls with Sales and Manufacturing VPs; Executive S&OP for major shifts
decision_types.clarity: ⚠️ Ambiguous — Patricia cannot drive unilaterally; formal path (Executive S&OP) is too slow; actual path is bilateral ad hoc

decision_types.forum: Supply Chain Leadership Meeting; within budget authority
decision_types.clarity: Clear

decision_types.forum: Supply Chain Leadership Meeting; Weekly Operations Review for urgent cases
decision_types.clarity: Clear — explicitly escalated to VP level by Directors regardless of dollar value

decision_types.forum: Executive S&OP; direct COO engagement with CFO visibility
decision_types.clarity: Clear threshold; ⚠️ decision documentation and cascade back to VP execution team noted as inconsistent

decision_types.forum: Executive S&OP
decision_types.clarity: Clear

decision_types.decision: Multi-year strategic supplier commitments (especially where quality issues co-exist)
decision_types.forum: Weekly Operations Review and/or direct escalation to COO
decision_types.clarity: ⚠️ Threshold is judgment-based, not codified — Patricia applies a 'would Marcus want to know' heuristic rather than a documented criterion

decision_types.decision: Sourcing contracts and order placement within existing supplier relationships (below $50M)
decision_types.forum: Weekly Procurement Team Meeting; bilateral with VP for strategic implications
decision_types.clarity: Clear

decision_types.forum: Supply Chain Leadership Meeting (authorization); Supplier Performance Review (evidence base)
decision_types.clarity: Clear — explicitly escalated even below dollar threshold

decision_types.decision: Corrective action plan authorization for suppliers
decision_types.forum: Monthly Supplier Performance Review
decision_types.clarity: Clear within supplier execution issues; ⚠️ contested root cause (supplier vs. Planning forecast) cannot close without VP escalation or bilateral resolution

decision_types.decision: Master schedule sequence changes within 2-week rolling window
decision_types.forum: Daily Production Stand-Up; direct authority, no escalation required
decision_types.clarity: Clear

decision_types.decision: Demand plan changes with commercial implication
decision_types.forum: Monthly S&OP — requires Sales alignment
decision_types.clarity: ⚠️ Sarah cannot close unilaterally; dependent on Sales VP presence and engagement in S&OP

decision_types.forum: Escalated from James Patel to Commodity Manager to David Okonkwo
decision_types.clarity: ⚠️ Approval path is clear but latency is operationally problematic — documented near-stoppage incident where 36-hour timeline was insufficient

decision_types.decision: Engineering disposition requests (tolerance relaxations, spec changes)
decision_types.forum: Ad hoc — no standing forum; Engineering liaison or direct contact with design engineer
decision_types.clarity: ⚠️ No standing cross-functional forum; David cannot compel Engineering to act; items sit in Engineering queue with no SLA — documented 4-month unresolved casting disposition

authority_tiers.tier: Manager / Senior Manager (Marcus Thompson, James Patel)

decision_types.decision: Order quantities and timing within existing contracts
decision_types.forum: Bi-weekly Materials Planning Sync; Weekly Procurement Team Meeting
decision_types.clarity: Clear

decision_types.decision: Short-term price negotiations (spot buys, quarterly adjustments) within approved relationships
decision_types.forum: Buyer authority; results reported up at Weekly Procurement Team Meeting
decision_types.clarity: Clear

decision_types.decision: Local expedite requests within approved suppliers and contracts (plant)
decision_types.forum: Plant-level; James Patel's authority
decision_types.clarity: Clear

decision_types.forum: Joint call between James Patel and Director of Operations
decision_types.clarity: Clear

decision_types.forum: Daily 7am Stand-Up; James Patel's authority
decision_types.clarity: Clear

decision_types.forum: ⚠️ Not Manager-level — must escalate to Director; Marcus Thompson explicitly confirmed this boundary
decision_types.clarity: Clear boundary; ⚠️ 3-4 week decision cycle for non-emergency new qualification creates latency risk when supplier financial stress is early-stage

section_4_decision_authority_map.authority_distribution_note: The decision authority architecture is broadly coherent at the VP and Director tiers, with reasonably clear thresholds on financial delegation. However, two structural weaknesses are evident. First, cross-functional decisions — particularly those requiring Engineering commitment or Sales acceptance of delivery changes — have no formal authority mechanism below the VP level; they depend on bilateral relationships and informal influence, creating latency and unresolved persistence risk. Second, the informal escalation heuristic Patricia uses with the COO ('would Marcus want to know') is not documented or shared with Directors, meaning escalation calibration below the VP tier is uncodified. The semiconductor/electronics procurement commodity (not directly interviewed in this cohort) was identified in the research brief as strategically elevated post-COVID; based on the pattern observed across metals and direct materials, it is highly probable that electronics/semiconductor escalation authority is similarly concentrated at Director/VP level with compressed timelines creating the same latency risk — this should be explicitly confirmed in the next interview wave.
section_4_decision_authority_map.semiconductor_note: ⚠️ The Manager, Commodity Purchasing (Electronics/Semiconductors) was not among the five interviewees in this cohort. Given the known strategic elevation of this category post-COVID and the documented latency risks in the premium freight and dual-sourcing escalation paths, the semiconductor decision authority pattern should be treated as an explicit gap and prioritized in subsequent interviews.

section_5_cross_functional_seams.title: Section 5: Cross-Functional Seams & Handoff Points

seams_table.nature_of_handoff: Schedule attainment accountability is shared — Supply Chain owns material delivery and planning; Manufacturing owns equipment, labor, and plant schedule changes. Late schedule changes by Manufacturing flow back as Supply Chain KPI misses.
seams_table.sc_interface_owner: Sarah Martinez (Director, Production Planning) at Director level; James Patel at plant level
seams_table.risk_or_tension: On-time delivery to plants is a joint KPI with no clear RACI for root cause attribution when misses occur. Sarah and James both noted that Manufacturing-driven schedule changes hit their scorecards. Daily stand-up surfaces the tension but does not resolve accountability.

seams_table.nature_of_handoff: Supplier specification changes, tolerance dispositions, and drawing revisions require Engineering sign-off before Procurement can resolve supplier quality nonconformances. Engineering has a single procurement liaison but no standing joint decision forum.
seams_table.sc_interface_owner: David Okonkwo (Director, Procurement) — ad hoc engagement via Engineering liaison or direct contact
seams_table.risk_or_tension: ⚠️ Critical gap. A 4-month unresolved casting disposition (documented by David Okonkwo) demonstrates that Engineering's queue has no SLA for supply chain-initiated requests. The $200-300K annual cost of the unresolved item is distributed across cost centers, masking the true financial impact and removing the financial trigger for escalation. This is the most structurally significant cross-functional seam identified in this interview cohort.

seams_table.nature_of_handoff: Demand plan inputs from Sales drive the S&OP process; Sales must accept or reject delivery push-outs when shortages occur. Sarah Martinez cannot close demand plan changes or customer delivery commitments without Sales VP involvement.
seams_table.sc_interface_owner: Sarah Martinez (S&OP facilitator); Patricia Chen (VP-to-VP for contested decisions)
seams_table.risk_or_tension: Monthly S&OP is the designated forum for this interface but is underperforming under operational pressure. Patricia escalates to bilateral VP calls as a workaround. Risk: Sales-driven demand volatility (e.g., late forecast changes) directly causes inventory days on hand misses that are measured against Supply Chain, with no formal accountability mechanism for Sales-side forecast accuracy.

seams_table.nature_of_handoff: Finance owns landed cost measurement and reporting; Procurement owns the cost levers. Cost allocation methodology creates recurring tension. Above-threshold supplier contracts require CFO visibility alongside COO.
seams_table.sc_interface_owner: Patricia Chen (VP level for major decisions); David Okonkwo (Director level for contract financials)
seams_table.risk_or_tension: The $200-300K annual cost of the unresolved casting nonconformance is distributed across cost centers in a way that makes it invisible as a supply chain issue — acknowledged explicitly by David. This cost-visibility gap is a structural Finance-Supply Chain seam that removes financial pressure from cross-functional resolution of supplier issues.

seams_table.nature_of_handoff: Central supply chain sets policy, demand plans, and supplier contracts; James Patel executes at the plant and escalates upward. Premium freight above $15K must route through central Commodity Manager and Procurement Director before authorization.
seams_table.sc_interface_owner: James Patel (plant); relevant Commodity Manager and David Okonkwo (central)
seams_table.risk_or_tension: ⚠️ The $15K premium freight threshold has not been formally reviewed since it was set and is operationally insufficient for air freight scenarios. A documented near-stoppage event resulted from approval latency in the central escalation path. The threshold itself is a policy decision that sits with central Procurement — James cannot change it — and despite being raised at the quarterly Plant Supply Chain Review, it has not been assigned a formal owner or timeline.

section_5_cross_functional_seams.critical_dependencies_note: The most consequential cross-functional dependency identified is the Procurement-Engineering interface on supplier specification decisions: there is no standing forum, no SLA, and no escalation trigger short of a line-down event, meaning issues can persist for months while costs accumulate invisibly. The second most significant dependency is the Supply Chain-Sales interface within S&OP: when the monthly working session underperforms, Patricia defaults to bilateral VP calls, but there is no documented fallback governance mechanism. The plant-central premium freight threshold is a lower-magnitude but immediately actionable structural fix.

section_6_executive_narrative.title: Section 6: Executive Narrative & Diagnostic Priorities
section_6_executive_narrative.how_decisions_actually_flow: The supply chain function operates with a coherent tiered governance architecture: a quarterly Executive S&OP for strategic framework decisions, a monthly Supply Chain Leadership Meeting for sub-function accountability and structural decisions, a monthly S&OP working session for demand-supply alignment, and high-cadence operational forums (bi-weekly Materials Planning Sync, daily Production Stand-Up) for tactical execution. Patricia Chen holds clear authority on the supply chain side, closes decisions in the monthly S&OP, and escalates to COO with well-calibrated judgment. Below the VP level, Directors have clear financial delegation thresholds and well-understood escalation paths for supplier and sourcing decisions. However, the system's load-bearing assumption — that cross-functional alignment happens through formal forums — breaks down at two points: the monthly S&OP working session underperforms under operational pressure, pushing alignment work into ad hoc bilateral calls at the VP level; and cross-functional decisions requiring Engineering commitment have no formal mechanism at any level below the VP, creating chronic unresolved issues. Decision documentation and cascade from verbal agreement to written execution direction is inconsistently practiced across the function.
what_is_working_well: Clear financial delegation: The $50M VP threshold and the plant-level $15K premium freight threshold are understood and consistently applied across the Director and Manager tiers. Marcus Thompson and James Patel both articulated their boundaries accurately without ambiguity.

High-cadence operational forums function well: The bi-weekly Materials Planning Sync and the daily Production Stand-Up operate with lean discipline — short, structured, action-oriented. These are the most effective decision forums in the architecture for their intended scope.

Supplier disqualification and quality escalation authority is clean: Patricia's unilateral authority on controlled shipping and disqualification is explicitly designed for speed and is well understood by the function. This is correctly delegated given the cost of delay in quality escapes.

The Monthly Supplier Performance Review provides a structured cross-sub-function accountability mechanism at the Director level — the forum is well-structured and closes real decisions on supplier execution issues within the Procurement and Quality teams' combined authority.
gaps_overlaps_bottlenecks: ⚠️ Monthly S&OP working session has ambiguous ownership and degrades under pressure: Patricia explicitly identifies this forum as underperforming — no designated chair with accountability for forum effectiveness, attendance degrades when operational pressure is high, and substitutes without decision authority attend. The result is that the primary cross-functional demand-supply alignment mechanism defaults to bilateral VP calls. This is not a resource problem; it is a governance design problem.

⚠️ Procurement-Engineering interface has no formal decision mechanism: There is no standing forum, no SLA, and no escalation trigger between Procurement and Engineering below the VP level. David Okonkwo's documented 4-month unresolved casting disposition — costing $200-300K annually — persists because the cost is distributed across cost centers and the only escalation trigger is a line-down event. Engineering's procurement liaison is a single point of contact, not a governance mechanism.

⚠️ KPI ownership is widely shared but RACI is not formally documented: Schedule attainment, shortage incidents, OTD to plants, and inventory days on hand all have multiple contributors and no documented RACI distinguishing 'accountable' from 'contributes to.' Sarah Martinez owns schedule attainment 'on paper' but has limited unilateral control over it. This creates scorecard misalignment under pressure and contested accountability during post-mortems.

⚠️ Decision documentation from verbal alignment to written execution direction is structurally weak: Patricia identified this pattern directly — decisions made verbally in meetings (including with the COO) do not consistently produce written direction that execution teams can act on. The stamping supplier dual-sourcing case, where a quality-milestone condition was verbally agreed but not documented or owned, is a concrete example. This is a systemic governance hygiene issue, not an isolated incident.

⚠️ Plant-central premium freight threshold ($15K) is operationally stale and has no owner for review: The threshold has not been formally reviewed in at least two years; air freight on urgent expedites routinely exceeds it; a documented near-stoppage event resulted from approval latency; and despite being raised at the Quarterly Plant Supply Chain Review, it has not been assigned a formal action owner with a date. The threshold itself is a low-complexity fix that has been stuck because of the same action-ownership gap that characterizes the Quarterly Plant Review outputs more broadly.

prioritized_areas_for_investigation.area: Monthly S&OP working session — governance redesign
prioritized_areas_for_investigation.rationale: This is the single most structurally consequential gap identified. The forum is the intended load-bearing mechanism for cross-functional demand-supply alignment, but it has no designated chair, degrades under pressure, and has forced alignment work into ad hoc bilateral VP calls. Fixing this — by assigning explicit ownership, accountability criteria, and a decision-authority charter — would have the highest leverage on overall decision quality across the function. Interviews from Sales Planning and Manufacturing Planning directors should be prioritized to confirm the bilateral call pattern from their side.

The absence of a standing forum with Engineering for supplier specification decisions is creating persistent, financially invisible issue accumulation. The casting disposition case is likely not isolated — it represents a structural pattern where Engineering's queue has no SLA for supply chain requests. Mapping the full universe of open Engineering-dependent supplier issues would establish the true scope; interviews with Engineering leadership are required to understand the barrier from their side.

Multiple high-visibility KPIs (schedule attainment, shortage incidents, OTD to plants, inventory days on hand) have multiple contributors without a documented RACI distinguishing accountability from contribution. This creates contested post-mortems and diffuse corrective action ownership. A structured RACI exercise across the six sub-functions, anchored to the KPI Ownership Matrix in this brief, would resolve this relatively quickly.

prioritized_areas_for_investigation.area: Decision documentation discipline — from verbal alignment to written execution direction
prioritized_areas_for_investigation.rationale: Patricia identified this as a self-aware gap; Marcus Thompson's escalation resolution pattern ('same day or next day' directional guidance, but formal decisions dependent on Marcus Webb's calendar) suggests it manifests at multiple tiers. A lightweight action-documentation protocol for decisions made in the Supply Chain Leadership Meeting and Monthly S&OP would address the most high-impact instances without bureaucratic overhead.

prioritized_areas_for_investigation.area: Plant-central escalation authority review — premium freight threshold and Quarterly Plant Review action ownership
prioritized_areas_for_investigation.rationale: The $15K premium freight threshold is an immediately actionable structural fix with documented operational risk. Broader plant-central governance — specifically the absence of named action owners between quarterly reviews — represents the same documentation and accountability gap observed centrally, but with compounded risk given the time-sensitivity of plant-floor operations. Interviewing Plant Managers at additional assembly plants would confirm whether this pattern is systemic across the plant network.
